items.title: When a French City Becomes a Stage: The Festival d'Avignon Returns This July
items.body:
Each July, the medieval city of Avignon in southern France transforms into one of the world's great open-air theaters. The **Festival d'Avignon**, founded in 1947 by the visionary director Jean Vilar, returns for its 2026 edition — and for a few intense weeks, the stone courtyards, chapels, and squares of a UNESCO-listed city belong to live performance.

## A festival with two faces

What makes Avignon singular is its split personality:

- **The "In"** — the official, curated program. Its most iconic venue is the **Cour d'honneur of the Palais des Papes** (the Papal Palace), where large-scale productions play against a backdrop of towering medieval walls under the night sky.
- **The "Off"** — a sprawling fringe festival that erupts across the city at the same time, with hundreds of independent companies performing in every available room, cellar, and courtyard. Streets fill with actors handing out flyers, and the whole city hums with theatrical energy.

Together they make Avignon less a single event than an ecosystem — the establishment and the experimental, side by side.

## Why it still matters

In an age of streaming and screens, Avignon is a defiant celebration of the **irreplaceable, communal experience of live theater**. It has long been a laboratory for new writing, physical theater, dance, and boundary-pushing staging, and its programming often reflects the political and social questions of the moment. A hit at Avignon can launch a production onto international stages; a bold experiment can redefine what theater is allowed to be.

## The atmosphere

Beyond the shows, the festival is a summer pilgrimage. The Provençal heat, the crowded café terraces, the debates spilling out of late-night performances — it's as much a cultural gathering as a program of plays. Audiences don't just watch; they argue, discover, and return year after year.

For anyone who loves the stage, Avignon in July is a reminder of something simple and powerful: put remarkable performers in a remarkable place, gather an audience under the stars, and the oldest art form in the world still has the power to astonish.

items.title: July 1 Resets Australia's Money Clock: What the New Financial Year Means for Your Career
items.body:
While much of the world treats January as the year's fresh start, in Australia the truly consequential reset arrives on **July 1**. That's when a new **financial year** begins — and for workers, it's one of the most important dates on the professional calendar.

## Why July 1 hits your paycheck

The start of the financial year is when a cluster of workplace and money changes typically take effect:

- **Minimum and award wages.** Following the Fair Work Commission's annual review, updated wage rates generally apply from the first full pay period on or after July 1 — directly affecting millions of workers.
- **Superannuation.** Australia's compulsory retirement contribution, the Superannuation Guarantee, has now reached **12% of ordinary earnings**, a level phased in over recent years. The new financial year is the moment to check your fund, fees, and contribution strategy.
- **Tax settings.** Income tax thresholds, offsets, and reporting all reset with the new year, making early July prime time to get your affairs in order.

## Turn the reset into a career move

A new financial year isn't just an accounting event — it's a natural checkpoint for your working life. Use it to:

1. **Benchmark your pay.** If minimum and award rates have risen, ask whether your own salary has kept pace with the market and your value.
2. **Audit your super.** Small differences in fees and fund performance compound enormously over a career. A 20-minute review now can be worth thousands later.
3. **Set concrete goals.** Treat July as a mid-year performance review with yourself: What do you want the *next* twelve months to deliver — a promotion, a new skill, a role change?
4. **Prepare for reviews.** Many organizations align budgets and planning to the financial year, meaning July is often when raises, restructures, and new headcount are decided. Be ready to make your case.

## The bigger lesson

Australia's July reset is a useful idea for workers anywhere: don't wait for January to take stock of your career and finances. Pick a recurring date, review your pay, your savings, and your goals — and treat the second half of the year as a deliberate fresh start rather than a drift toward December.

items.title: Mount Fuji's Gates Open on July 1: How to Climb Japan's Icon the Smart Way
items.body:
Every summer, Japan's most famous silhouette turns from a postcard into a destination. On **July 1**, the popular **Yoshida Trail** on the Yamanashi side officially opens for the season, marking the traditional start of the short window when climbing Mount Fuji is sanctioned and supported.

If summiting the 3,776-meter volcano is on your list, here's how to do it without turning a bucket-list moment into a miserable one.

## Know the season

Mount Fuji is only open for a matter of weeks, roughly **early July through early September**. The Yoshida Trail typically opens first on July 1; the other main routes (Subashiri, Gotemba, and Fujinomiya) generally open a little later in the month. Outside this window, the mountain huts close, buses stop, and the climb becomes genuinely dangerous.

## Expect crowd controls

In recent years, authorities have introduced measures to curb overtourism and improve safety, including:

- An **entry fee** and, on the Yoshida Trail, a **daily cap on climbers**.
- **Reservation systems** for trail access and mountain huts.
- Gates that may **close overnight** to discourage risky, sleepless "bullet climbing."

Rules and prices are adjusted year to year, so confirm the current requirements on official prefectural sources before you go — and book huts early, because they sell out.

## Climb it well

1. **Don't rush the summit.** Altitude sickness is the most common reason climbs fail. Pace yourself and consider spending a night at a hut around the 8th station.
2. **Layer up.** It can be near freezing at the top even in midsummer, with fierce wind.
3. **Respect *goraiko*.** The famous sunrise from the summit is unforgettable — but plan timing carefully and never sacrifice safety to catch it.
4. **Pack out everything.** Fuji is a sacred site and a UNESCO World Heritage property; there are no trash bins.

## A mountain worth the effort

There's a well-known Japanese saying: a wise person climbs Fuji once, but only a fool climbs it twice. Whether or not you agree, the July opening is your cue — the gates are up, the huts are lit, and Japan's icon is, briefly, yours to climb.
